mnemonics: Think of the gradient as a multi-dimensional slope indicator: just as a single derivative points uphill in 1D, the gradient vector points in the steepest ascent direction for a scalar function. For a vector-valued function, you stack these gradients into the Jacobian matrix, forming a 'directional derivative guide' for each output component.
keyPoints: The gradient of a scalar-valued function f(x, y, z) is a vector (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z) pointing in the direction of greatest increase.
For a vector-valued function F(x, y, z) = [P(x,y,z), Q(x,y,z), R(x,y,z)], the derivative is the Jacobian matrix, which has the gradients of P, Q, and R as its rows.
The Jacobian matrix represents the best linear approximation of the function near a point, generalizing the concept of a derivative to higher dimensions.
commonMistakes: Confusing the gradient (for scalar functions) with the Jacobian (for vector functions). The gradient is a vector, the Jacobian is a matrix.
Forgetting that the gradient vector points in the direction of steepest ascent, not descent. The negative of the gradient points downhill.
Miscalculating partial derivatives, especially when applying the chain rule in multiple dimensions.
